title: What Should Be Close in Representation Space?
learningObjectives: Articulate what it means for two points to be 'close' in a learned representation space
Distinguish metric choices from learning objectives
Explain why the choice of what to make close is a design decision, not a mathematical inevitability
Connect proximity criteria to downstream tasks and interpretability goals

whyThisLesson.text: Every representation learning method implicitly or explicitly decides: which inputs should map to nearby vectors? This decision shapes everything downstream — retrieval, clustering, classification, and interpretability. Before studying contrastive learning's specific answer, we must understand the space of possible answers and why the choice is non-trivial.

sections.bridgeFrom: Phase 5 concluded with set encoders that aggregate information across multiple members. Now we ask: once we have representations, what geometric relationships should hold between them? The answer determines which downstream tasks the representations will support.

intuition.analogy: Imagine organising a library. You could sort books alphabetically by title, by author, by genre, by reading level, by the emotion they evoke, or by the year they were published. Each ordering makes different queries easy and others hard. 'Closeness' in the library corresponds to proximity in representation space.
intuition.analogyLimit: In a library, you can only impose one physical ordering. In representation space, the geometry can simultaneously encode multiple relationships — but usually at the cost of some relationships being encoded less faithfully than others.
intuition.text: A representation space is a metric space where distance (or angle) signals similarity. The central design question is: similarity with respect to what? Pixel similarity? Semantic meaning? Task relevance? Causal structure? Different choices lead to representations that excel at different things. Contrastive learning operationalises this choice through the selection of positive and negative pairs.

toyExample.description: Consider 6 images: two cats (one fluffy, one shaved), two dogs (one fluffy, one shaved), one wolf (fluffy), one sheep (fluffy).

steps.action: Define proximity by texture: group fluffy animals together
steps.result: Cat-fluffy, dog-fluffy, wolf, sheep cluster; cat-shaved and dog-shaved cluster apart
steps.insight: Representations encode texture well, but species identity is scrambled

steps.action: Define proximity by species: group cats together regardless of texture
steps.result: Both cats cluster; both dogs cluster; wolf and sheep are separate
steps.insight: Representations encode species identity; texture variation within species is ignored

steps.result: Species-based proximity gives perfect retrieval; texture-based gives 50% accuracy
steps.insight: The choice of proximity criterion directly determines task performance — there is no universally correct choice

visual.question: How does changing the proximity criterion change which points cluster together?
visual.description: Arrange points in 2D vector space and toggle between different labelling schemes to see how the same data produces different cluster structures depending on what you define as 'similar'

technical.text: A proximity criterion specifies a mapping from pairs of inputs (x, x') to a scalar similarity score s(x, x') ∈ ℝ. Training a representation model to respect this criterion means learning f such that d(f(x), f(x')) is small when s(x, x') is large, and large otherwise. The choice of d (Euclidean, cosine, Manhattan) interacts with the criterion. Most contrastive methods use cosine similarity after L2 normalisation, which means learning directions rather than magnitudes. Three canonical categories of proximity criterion: (1) augmentation invariance — same image after different crops should be close; (2) label equivalence — same class label implies proximity; (3) semantic equivalence — same meaning regardless of surface form.

definitions.definition: A function specifying which pairs of inputs should be mapped to nearby representations; the core design decision of any representation learning method
The metric or inner-product structure of the space into which inputs are embedded; governs which downstream operations (nearest-neighbour, linear probing) are meaningful
The proximity criterion that treats two differently-augmented versions of the same data point as a positive pair; encourages representations to ignore augmentation-induced variation

mathematics.label: Proximity criterion formalisation

symbols.x^+: positive input (should be close to anchor)
symbols.x^-: negative input (should be far from anchor)

mathematics.plainExplanation: This generic contrastive objective pushes positive pairs closer and negative pairs further apart in representation space.
mathematics.whyNeeded: Formalises the proximity criterion as an optimisation target so it can be implemented in a neural network
mathematics.ifRemoved: Without this objective, the encoder has no incentive to organise the representation space according to any particular proximity criterion
mathematics.encourages: Representations where the chosen similarity criterion is reflected in geometric proximity
mathematics.doesNotGuarantee: That the proximity criterion captures semantically meaningful relationships; the criterion itself must be carefully chosen

methodComparison.question: How does the proximity criterion differ between augmentation-based and label-based contrastive learning?

methods.name: Augmentation-based (SimCLR)
methods.answer: Positive pairs are two augmented views of the same image. The criterion is augmentation-invariance: what remains stable across crops, flips, and colour jitter is treated as signal.

methods.name: Label-based (SupCon)
methods.answer: Positive pairs are all images sharing the same class label. The criterion is class-equivalence: intra-class variation is treated as noise, inter-class variation as signal.

methodComparison.practicalImplication: Augmentation-based learning can be applied without labels but the proximity criterion is defined by the augmentation policy, which may or may not align with semantic meaning. Label-based learning requires labels but defines proximity more directly in terms of task-relevant categories.

evidence.claim: The choice of augmentation strategy in self-supervised learning determines which features the model learns to encode
evidence.label: [Paper Finding]
evidence.paraphrase: Chen et al. showed that removing colour jitter from the augmentation pipeline caused SimCLR to rely heavily on colour statistics, demonstrating that the proximity criterion (via augmentation choice) directly controls which features are encoded

failureModes.name: Proximity criterion misalignment
failureModes.mechanism: The chosen proximity criterion does not align with the downstream task's notion of similarity
failureModes.symptom: High contrastive training accuracy but poor downstream task performance
failureModes.diagnosticTest: Compare nearest-neighbour accuracy in representation space against label-based accuracy; large gap indicates misalignment
failureModes.mitigation: Audit augmentation choices or label definitions against the downstream task's similarity requirements
failureModes.setconcaRelevance: SetConCA defines proximity via semantic co-occurrence in sets; this may not align with interpretability-relevant notions of feature similarity

setconcaConnection.relevance: SetConCA must choose which representations should be close: tokens within a semantically coherent set, or tokens sharing a feature regardless of set membership
setconcaConnection.whichPart: The contrastive regularisation component of SetConCA
setconcaConnection.whichDesignChoice: Whether to use set membership, token-level feature similarity, or both as the proximity criterion
setconcaConnection.whichFailureExplained: If the proximity criterion does not match the interpretability goal, the resulting representations may be geometrically clean but semantically opaque
setconcaConnection.proposedExperiment: Train SetConCA with three different proximity criteria (set membership, feature label, augmentation) and compare the resulting representations on both retrieval and linear probing tasks
setconcaConnection.alternativeExplanation: It is possible that no single proximity criterion captures all relevant relationships; multi-objective proximity criteria may be necessary

exercises.prompt: If you train a contrastive model where positive pairs are sentences with the same sentiment label, what will the representation space encode well, and what will it fail to encode?
exercises.reveal: The model will encode sentiment polarity well (positive/negative/neutral) and may cluster sentences by sentiment effectively. It will fail to encode syntactic structure, topic, or semantic content that does not correlate with sentiment. For example, 'I love dogs' and 'This film was fantastic' will be close, even though their topics differ.

exercises.prompt: Design a proximity criterion for learning representations of protein sequences that would be useful for predicting binding sites
exercises.reveal: A good criterion would treat proteins with the same binding site structure as positive pairs, regardless of overall sequence similarity. This might use experimentally verified binding site labels, or structural alignment scores as a proxy. Augmentations might include sequence mutations that preserve binding function.

masteryQuestions.prompt: Construct a dataset and proximity criterion where the representation space has perfect contrastive loss but is completely useless for a natural downstream task. Explain the mechanism.
rubric: Identifies that contrastive loss measures adherence to the chosen criterion, not downstream utility
Constructs a concrete example (e.g., proximity by file creation timestamp; downstream task is semantic similarity)
Explains that the problem is criterion-task misalignment, not model failure
Notes that evaluation must measure the right thing, not just training loss
masteryQuestions.badVersion: Contrastive learning fails when you choose the wrong loss function.
masteryQuestions.goodVersion: Consider images labelled by photographer name. A contrastive model trained with photographer identity as the proximity criterion achieves low loss — all photos by the same photographer cluster together. But this representation is useless for identifying objects in scenes, because the proximity criterion encodes photographer style, not object identity. The model succeeds at its stated objective while failing the downstream task.

researchWorksheet.claim: The choice of proximity criterion fully determines the structure of the learned representation space
researchWorksheet.requiredAssumptions: The encoder has sufficient capacity; the optimiser converges; the proximity criterion is consistent (no contradictory pairs)
researchWorksheet.evidenceProvided: Ablation studies in SimCLR show that augmentation choices qualitatively change what features are encoded
researchWorksheet.strongestAlternativeExplanation: Architecture inductive biases (e.g., convolutional structure) may impose structure on the representation space independent of the proximity criterion
researchWorksheet.missingControl: A control that holds architecture fixed while systematically varying only the proximity criterion across a range of downstream tasks
researchWorksheet.closestBaseline: Random encoder baselines that expose how much structure comes from architecture alone
researchWorksheet.falsifyingResult: If two encoders trained with identical proximity criteria but different architectures learn representations that are linearly indistinguishable, the architecture would be the dominant factor
researchWorksheet.setconcaImplication: SetConCA's contrastive component must be carefully designed so that its proximity criterion reflects interpretability-relevant similarity, not just set co-occurrence
researchWorksheet.proposedExperiment: Train 5 encoders with identical architectures but different proximity criteria (augmentation, label, set-membership, temporal adjacency, random) and compare downstream task performance across 10 tasks

bridgeToNext.text: Having established that the proximity criterion is a design decision, the next lesson examines the positive pair — the specific choice of which inputs should be made close — and how positive pair construction shapes what contrastive learning can and cannot learn.
